title: Ensure that the controller-manager.conf file permissions are set to 644 or more restrictive

input_row.title: Ensure that the controller-manager.conf file permissions are set to 644 or more restrictive
input_row.description: Ensure that the controller-manager.conf file has permissions of 644 or more restrictive.
input_row.rationale: The controller-manager.conf file is the kubeconfig file for the Controller Manager. You should restrict its file permissions to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be writable by only the administrators on the system.
input_row.audit: Run the following command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/controller-manager.conf Verify that the permissions are 644 or more restrictive.
input_row.remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the Control Plane node. For example, chmod 644 /etc/kubernetes/controller-manager.conf
input_row.default_value: By default, controller-manager.conf has permissions of 640 .

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The audit mentions using a command (`stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/controller-manager.conf`) to verify file permissions, which indicates that it requires access to the filesystem of the control plane node. However, since this is a configuration check that can be automated through Kubernetes API or kubectl commands, it can be classified as Automated.
- The rationale emphasizes the importance of file permissions for the integrity of the controller-manager configuration, which is a standard Kubernetes service property.
- The control is specific to the `controller-manager.conf` file, which is a known configuration file for the Kubernetes Controller Manager.

**PROGRAM NAME:** k8s_controller_manager_conf_permissions_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use the command `kubectl exec -n kube-system <controller-manager-pod-name> -- stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/controller-manager.conf` to check the permissions of the `controller-manager.conf` file.
- The expected output should be `644` or more restrictive (e.g., `600`).
- Pass criteria: If the output is `644`, `640`, `600`, or any other value that is more restrictive, the check passes.
- Fail criteria: If the output is `664`, `666`, or any other value that is less restrictive than `644`, the check fails.
